rationale: The page provides detailed profiles of two species: the Dhole (Asiatic Wild Dog) and the Alpine Musk Deer. It includes their conservation status (WPA, CITES, IUCN), habitat, and unique biological characteristics, which are highly relevant for environmental studies.
cleanedText: Dhole (Asiatic Wild Dog). Context: Dhole, once thought extinct in the region, has made a comeback in Assam's Kaziranga-Karbi Anglong Landscape (KKAL). Habitat: India, Cambodia, Nepal, Bhutan, Thailand, Central Asia, etc. Inhabits Eastern & Western Ghats, Assam, Meghalaya, West Bengal, Ladakh etc. Characteristics: Carnivores, resembles small wolf or domestic dog in size, hunt mainly in early morning or late evening. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; CITES: Appendix II; IUCN Status: EN (Endangered). Alpine Musk Deer. Context: Central Zoo Authority (CZA) report revealed a mix-up in identifying Alpine musk deer for conservation breeding, mistaking it for its Himalayan counterpart. Habitat: Temperate climate in alpine meadows, shrub lands, birch, blue pine and fir forests. Native species of India, Nepal, Bhutan and China. Characteristics: Solitary and shy, herbivorous. Unique with fangs and without antlers and gall bladder. Threat: Poaching for muskpod used in perfumes and traditional medicines. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; CITES: Appendix I; IUCN Status: EN (Endangered).
